action.hypothesis: After low-rank, discrete, bucketed, and gated branches all failed to beat the `K=24` frontier point, the next severe-stagnation move should test a fundamentally different routing mechanism without the low-rank trunk or codebook machinery. `routed` at `K=24, EF=12` may improve support allocation in the mid-K regime by learning input-dependent feature preference before hard top-k selection, potentially recovering a better FVU/K tradeoff than the recent losing families.
action.summary: Run a pure architecture comparison at `K=24, EF=12` with `routed` under the pinned recipe `OPTIMIZER=signum`, `LR=3.2e-3`, `AUXK_ALPHA=0.0`. This follows severe-stagnation policy by making another architecture-only jump rather than any scalar sweep or code repair, while staying anchored on the frontier-relevant mid-K slot.
action.self_review: I avoided another low-rank or discrete variant because those branches have already been sampled repeatedly and are not improving the frontier. I also avoided code edits because policy has been rejecting coupled repair rounds; this keeps the experiment architecture-only with all recipe fields pinned.
notes_to_memory: Round 54 continues severe-stagnation recovery with a pure `routed` architecture test after `gated` also failed badly at K=24.
Keep the run pinned at `K=24, EF=12, signum, LR=3.2e-3, AUXK_ALPHA=0.0` so architecture is the only primary variable.
`routed` tests whether simple input-dependent support routing, without low-rank trunks or discrete codebooks, works better in the mid-K regime than the recent losing branches.
next_hypotheses: If `routed` is competitive but slightly worse at K=24, test it at K=16 to see whether routing helps more under tighter sparsity.
If `routed` clearly loses at K=24, retire this branch and move to another fundamentally different built-in family rather than any scalar sweep.
If `routed` wins at K=24, follow with K=16 or K=32 to map whether the gain generalizes across the mid-K band.
